text.title: employee-value-proposition Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for employee-value-proposition. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Structure every EVP around exactly five pillars: Compensation, Benefits, Career, Work Environment, and Culture/Purpose. Each pillar must have at least three named proof points before external use.
rule.rationale: A pillar without proof points is aspirational copy. Glassdoor reviews immediately expose claims that do not match employee experience, creating recruiter credibility loss.

rule.statement: Follow the Discover-Define-Deliver-Measure sequence. Do not skip Discover (surveys + exit interviews + competitor analysis) before drafting the EVP statement.
rule.rationale: EVP drafted without Discover produces leadership talking points, not what employees actually experience. The gap between aspiration and reality is the leading cause of EVP backlash.

rule.statement: Reject any EVP pillar that cannot be backed by at least three named proof points from internal programs, budgets, or data. Replace unsupported claims with UNVERIFIED placeholders for human fill-in.
rule.rationale: Aspirational copy without evidence is detectable by candidates within the first 30 days and surfaces as Glassdoor reviews citing "company doesn't match the pitch." This destroys recruiter credibility.

rule.statement: Never invent diversity statistics. Use UNVERIFIED placeholders that humans fill from HRIS. Published false diversity metrics are a serious reputation and legal risk.
rule.rationale: DEI claims without internal data are reputation risk; agents synthesizing from public sources will produce percentages that contradict what employees see daily.

rule.statement: Run a post-pass stripping generic clichés ("innovative," "passionate," "fast-paced," "world-class") from every EVP draft.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to these phrases; they add word count with zero signal and are the most common reason candidates call EVPs "corporate speak."

rule.statement: If the Applies-If preconditions fail OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or to no methodology at all).
rule.rationale: Mis-application of a methodology produces worse artefacts than no methodology at all because downstream consumers trust the schema and act on bad data. The skip-record makes the non-application traceable so future audits can detect drift.
